reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Child-centred comprehensive schooling (Kockás könyv 2016); reduction of test-preparation burden on age-10 pupils; equitable access to high-quality peers regardless of family resources
reactions.interest: Grassroots education-reform movement: defence of common-track comprehensive schooling and reduction of family-resource-dependent stratification by age 10
reactions.public_good_frame: Early selection by proxy for family wealth reduces equity and concentrates pressure on children; postponing selection preserves developmental time and peer diversity
reactions.argument: S1 attempts to reform the intake mechanism without touching the structure — this is honest about what it can achieve: modest reweighting of selective seats toward high-ability low-SES applicants who already apply. We support the stated goal of weakening SES-skew in access. However, the scenario itself acknowledges that every proposed lever rests on weak or unverified evidence: no workable SES indicator exists at application in Hungary, any proxy is gameable, and a genuinely less-coachable assessment is unverified. More fundamentally: S1 leaves the sorting gate intact at age 10 and merely changes who passes through it. This does not address the core harm we identify — that early selection itself concentrates resources, reduces peer diversity, and pressures children by age 10 regardless of admission rule tweaks. The outreach commitment is welcome, but documented outreach is a credibility threshold, not proof that application rates from low-SES families will rise materially. S1 risks relabelling rather than reducing SES sorting. We can support S1 as an interim harm-reduction measure — it is better than unchanged rules — but only if it is explicitly paired with a credible path to structural change (S3) within a defined timeframe, and only if the outreach commitment includes published tracking of uptake elasticity by SES and ethnicity.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would move to full support if: (a) a pilot demonstration that the SES proxy is robust against gaming emerges within 24 months; AND (b) a psychometric pilot shows a genuinely less-coachable assessment can be designed and validated; AND (c) the reform is legally anchored as a transitional step toward comprehensive lower-secondary by age 14 within 10 years, with binding legislative language; AND (d) published annual data show that low-SES and Roma application rates to reformed selective places rise by at least 15% within 3 years of implementation. We would move to opposition if: the reform is presented as a permanent solution to selection inequality, or if non-state maintainers are permitted to opt out of the reformed admission rules, or if the SES proxy becomes a tool for informal sorting rather than formal reweighting.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Postponement of selection to age 14-15 as the core reform; rejection of early gatekeeping as incompatible with child development and equity; non-state maintainer plurality as a structural blocker to caps-based reform
reactions.interest: Grassroots education-reform movement: defence of common-track comprehensive schooling and reduction of family-resource-dependent stratification by age 10
reactions.public_good_frame: Phased shrinking of selective seats without abolishing them leaves the sorting mechanism intact and merely relocates it to non-state maintainers; this is structural evasion, not structural reform
reactions.argument: S2 acknowledges its own deepest problem: caps on state-maintained schools are 'directly contradicted by this scenario's own political-risks and uncertainties, which identify exactly [non-state offset] as the dominant risk.' We agree with this self-assessment. The scenario depends on an assumption — that non-state maintainers do not absorb displaced selective demand — that the scenario itself marks as near-certain to be violated. This is not a reform, it is a cost-transfer: it shifts selective intake from state to church and foundation schools while leaving the age-10 gate intact and the SES-sorting mechanism unchanged. Residual selective places in capped state schools become even more SES-skewed as seats shrink (fewer seats, same applicant pool). Meanwhile, the mobility-insurance asset that middle-class parents value is not removed; it is merely repackaged in non-state schools, which the scenario acknowledges can 'absorb selective demand and bypass consolidation.' We do not oppose phase-down as a tactic if it is bundled with legislative parity for non-state intake limits. But S2 as stated is a phase-down that leaves the sorting gate operational and merely evades it. The administrative complexity of running parallel regimes for a decade while this evasion unfolds is a cost to citizens and teachers without compensating benefit to equity. The peer-composition benefit for urban general schools is contested in the scenario itself and contingent on the non-state offset not occurring — which the scenario rates as near-certain. We oppose S2 because it is structural evasion dressed as structural reform.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would move to conditional support if: (a) legislative parity for non-state maintainer intake limits is adopted simultaneously with the state-school cap (timeline: year 1, not deferred); AND (b) a binding constitutional or legislative safeguard prevents legislative reversal of the cap within 15 years; AND (c) published annual data show that non-state selective seats do not increase by more than 5% annually during the phase-down; AND (d) the reform is explicitly anchored as a transitional step toward comprehensive lower-secondary to age 14 by year 10, not as a permanent endpoint. We would move to stronger opposition if: non-state maintainers are explicitly exempted from intake limits, or if the phase-down is presented as the final reform and structural change is ruled out, or if middle-class mobilisation succeeds in exempting prestigious state gimnaziums from the cap.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Hanushek & Woessmann 2006 (early tracking raises inequality without raising mean achievement); Polish 1999 comprehensive reform (Jakubowski et al. 2016); Finnish equity bundling (teacher quality + early support); Kockás könyv 2016 (comprehensive lower-secondary as core reform goal); 2026 workload/autonomy package (teacher conditions as non-negotiable for reform durability)
reactions.interest: Grassroots education-reform movement: defence of common-track comprehensive schooling to age 14-15; reduction of family-resource-dependent stratification; teacher autonomy and workload relief as prerequisites for successful inclusive instruction
reactions.public_good_frame: Early selection at age 10 is incompatible with equity and child development; postponing selection to age 14-15 widens peer diversity, reduces SES-outcome gradients, and protects childhood from family-resource gatekeeping; bundling with teacher-quality investment is non-negotiable for equity materialisation
reactions.argument: S3 is the core reform we advocate. The evidence is strong: Hanushek & Woessmann establish that early tracking raises inequality without raising mean achievement, and the Polish 1999 reform (Jakubowski et al. 2016) shows that structural delay of selection, bundled with teacher investment, can raise both equity and mean outcomes. We recognise that the within-decade peer-effect mechanism is contested and that political durability is a weak point — but these are reasons to invest heavily in implementation and teacher conditions, not reasons to abandon the goal. The scenario correctly identifies the binding constraints: (a) teacher supply and quality; (b) political ownership across electoral cycles; (c) the risk that sorting relocates rather than diminishes via non-state exit. We accept all three as real risks. On teacher supply: the Kockás könyv and our 2026 workload/autonomy package are explicit that comprehensive instruction cannot succeed without decoupling teacher accountability from high-stakes testing, reducing administrative burden, and raising compensation to retain and attract talent to disadvantaged schools. We will not accept S3 as a reform unless these conditions are met. On political durability: we support constitutional or cross-party embedding of the reform to survive electoral cycles. On sorting relocation: we support simultaneous non-state intake parity (as in S2 condition), but S3 differs because it removes the state-school sorting gate entirely — relocating sorting is still a problem, but it is a problem S1 and S2 also face, and S3 at least removes the state machinery of early selection. We support S3 as the primary reform, bundled with teacher-investment and accountability-decoupling as non-negotiable conditions.
reactions.condition_to_change: We move to conditional support (from support) if: (a) the bundled teacher-retraining and accountability-decoupling roadmap is not co-authored with teacher unions before piloting, or is not legally protected from unilateral reversal; OR (b) the pilot phase (year 2-5) does not include comparison-group evaluation and is not genuinely formative (i.e. permits learning and adjustment, not just go/no-go); OR (c) non-state intake parity is not legislated simultaneously (timeline: year 1-2); OR (d) middle-class mobilisation succeeds in carving out exemptions for 'elite' state schools before the structural change takes effect. We would move to opposition if: (a) the reform is advanced without teacher-investment bundling (i.e. comprehensive instruction is imposed on teachers without workload reduction or accountability reform); OR (b) a Poland-style reversal occurs and the sorting gate is reinstated before 10-year outcomes data are available; OR (c) comprehensive lower-secondary is implemented without equivalent early diagnostic and special-education support systems (copying the Finnish model is non-negotiable).

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Rejection of compensation-without-structural-change as a model for equity; Hanushek & Woessmann 2006 (early tracking harms equity regardless of downstream compensation); Kockás könyv 2016 (structural reform, not palliative funding, as the core goal)
reactions.interest: Grassroots education-reform movement: defence of common-track comprehensive schooling and reduction of family-resource-dependent stratification by age 10; rejection of schemes that entrench early selection while funding its symptoms
reactions.public_good_frame: Compensation without structural change masks rather than offsets the effects of early selection; it legitimises the sorting gate by attempting to soften its impact, and it diverts resources from the root cause — the age-10 selection itself
reactions.argument: S4 is fundamentally a harm-mitigation scheme presented as a reform. The scenario itself states that its core claim — that compensation can 'offset not merely mask' early-sorting effects — is 'unverifiable ex ante' and has no country evidence at Hungary's selection age. This is honest, but it also means S4 is a gamble with high stakes: if compensation merely masks rather than offsets, resources flow to a permanent palliative rather than structural change. We reject this trade-off. The evidence is strong (Hanushek & Woessmann 2006) that early tracking raises inequality regardless of downstream compensation. Portugal's TEIP reform is moderately evidenced and notable, but Portugal did not alter its selection age — it compensated within a tracked system. Hungary's selection age (10-12) is earlier than Portugal's, so transfer is untested. More fundamentally: S4 legitimises early selection by attempting to soften its edges. It says: 'We will keep the gate at age 10, but fund the schools on the losing side.' This is a concession to early selection, not a challenge to it. It also faces a severe durability risk: the scenario acknowledges that 'ring-fenced equity funding is the first cut in fiscal consolidation, undermining durability.' This is the pattern we have observed in Hungary — education funding is volatile, and equity allocations are the first to go when budgets tighten. S4 is politically easier in the short term because it does not mobilise middle-class resistance; but it is politically fragile and educationally insufficient. We oppose S4 because it trades structural reform for a palliative that history shows will be cut. We will support targeted funding for disadvantaged schools only as a complement to S3 (structural reform), not as a substitute for it.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would move to conditional support (from opposition) if S4 were explicitly paired with a binding legislative commitment to structural change (S3) by year 7, with defined timelines and milestones. In that case, S4 could serve as a transitional harm-reduction while structural reform is prepared. However, even with this condition, we would require: (a) a constitutional or legislative supermajority lock on TEIP-style funding allocations to prevent cuts during fiscal consolidation; (b) published annual data showing that compensated general schools narrow the SES-achievement gap by at least 2 percentile points annually relative to uncompensated schools; (c) simultaneous investment in teacher supply and workload reduction in disadvantaged schools to prevent S4 from being an unfunded mandate; (d) explicit annual reporting on whether compensation is offsetting or merely masking early-sorting effects, with decision rules for pivot if masking is confirmed. We would move to stronger opposition if: (a) S4 is presented as a permanent endpoint rather than a transitional measure, or (b) the teacher-shortage constraint causes compensatory staffing to fail in exactly the disadvantaged schools S4 targets, or (c) funding is cut before outcome data are available to evaluate whether offset or masking is occurring.
